nodes.label: If P, then Q: P is the sufficient condition, Q is the necessary condition.

nodes.label: Contrapositive: If not Q, then not P (logically equivalent to the original statement).

nodes.label: Fallacy of Denying the Antecedent: Assuming 'Not P' implies 'Not Q'.

nodes.label: Fallacy of Affirming the Consequent: Assuming 'Q' implies 'P'.

nodes.label: All: 100% inclusion; no exceptions allowed.

nodes.label: Some: At least one; does not imply 'most' or 'all'.

nodes.label: None: 0% inclusion; mutually exclusive.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Most: More than 50%; implies 'some'.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Many: Vague term; usually implies a significant number but lacks mathematical precision.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Negating 'All' results in 'At least one is not'.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Negating 'Some' results in 'None'.

nodes.label: Correct inferences often rephrase explicit text using synonyms.

nodes.label: Avoid 'word matching' traps where options use text words but change the meaning.

nodes.label: Combining two separate sentences to form a single logical conclusion.

nodes.label: Inferring the author's stance based on loaded adjectives and adverbs.

nodes.label: Distinguishing between objective reporting and subjective commentary.

nodes.label: Extreme language: Words like 'always', 'never', 'only' often make an inference incorrect.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Misattribution: Assigning an opinion to the wrong person or group mentioned in the text.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Out of Scope: Introducing information that is plausible but not supported by the passage.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Reversal: Stating the opposite of what the text implies.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Unsupported Comparison: Comparing two things when the text does not establish a relationship between them.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Temporal shift: Confusing past, present, and future tenses.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Causality error: Assuming correlation equals causation without explicit textual support.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Premises are the evidence; the conclusion is the claim derived from evidence.

nodes.label: Indicator words for premises: 'because', 'since', 'given that', 'for'.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Indicator words for conclusions: 'therefore', 'thus', 'consequently', 'it follows that'.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: An assumption is an unstated premise required for the conclusion to hold.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: The 'Negation Test': If the negation of an assumption destroys the argument, it is a necessary assumption.

nodes.label: Strengthening: Adding information that makes the conclusion more likely to be true.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Weakening: Adding information that makes the conclusion less likely to be true.

nodes.label: Avoiding 'false analogies' where the surface similarity masks a structural difference.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Ad Hominem: Attacking the person rather than the argument.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Circular Reasoning: The conclusion is merely a restatement of the premise.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: False Dilemma: Assuming only two options exist when more are possible.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Hasty Generalization: Drawing a broad conclusion from a small or unrepresentative sample.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Post Hoc Ergo Propter Hoc: Assuming that because B followed A, A caused B.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Straw Man: Misrepresenting an opponent's argument to make it easier to attack.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Appeal to Authority: Assuming a claim is true simply because an authority figure said it.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Appeal to Popularity: Assuming a claim is true because many people believe it.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Composition/Division: Assuming what is true of the parts is true of the whole (or vice versa).
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Equivocation: Using the same term in two different senses within an argument.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Ignoring Alternatives: Failing to consider other explanations for a phenomenon.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Confusing Necessary and Sufficient conditions.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Misinterpreting statistics: Confusing absolute numbers with percentages.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Survivor Bias: Drawing conclusions from a set of data that has been filtered.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Sunk Cost Fallacy: Continuing an endeavor because of past investment rather than future utility.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Gambler's Fallacy: Believing that past independent events influence future probabilities.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Red Herring: Introducing irrelevant information to distract from the main argument.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Slippery Slope: Assuming a small first step will inevitably lead to a chain of negative events.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Tu Quoque: Deflecting criticism by accusing the critic of the same fault.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Burden of Proof: Shifting the responsibility of proof to the wrong party.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: No True Scotsman: Redefining a group to exclude counterexamples.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Genetic Fallacy: Judging an idea based on its origin rather than its merit.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Middle Ground: Assuming the truth must lie exactly between two extreme positions.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Special Pleading: Applying a double standard to an argument.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Loaded Question: Asking a question that contains a controversial assumption.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Fallacy of Composition: Assuming the whole has the same properties as its parts.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Fallacy of Division: Assuming the parts have the same properties as the whole.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Texas Sharpshooter: Cherry-picking data to support a conclusion after the fact.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Personal Incredulity: Assuming something is false because it is difficult to understand.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Ambiguity: Using language that can be interpreted in multiple ways to obscure the argument.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Inconsistency: Holding two contradictory beliefs simultaneously.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Ignoring the counter-evidence: Focusing only on data that supports the conclusion.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: False Equivalence: Treating two different things as equal without sufficient evidence.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Confirmation Bias: Seeking information that confirms existing beliefs.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Availability Heuristic: Overestimating the importance of information that is easily recalled.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Anchoring Bias: Relying too heavily on the first piece of information encountered.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Dunning-Kruger Effect: Overestimating one's own competence in a specific area.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Fundamental Attribution Error: Overemphasizing personality traits over situational factors.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Self-Serving Bias: Attributing successes to internal factors and failures to external ones.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Halo Effect: Allowing one positive trait to influence the perception of other traits.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Hindsight Bias: Believing an event was predictable after it has occurred.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Framing Effect: Being influenced by how information is presented rather than the information itself.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Optimism Bias: Believing one is less at risk of negative events than others.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Pessimism Bias: Overestimating the likelihood of negative outcomes.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Status Quo Bias: Preferring things to stay the same to avoid change.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Bandwagon Effect: Adopting beliefs because others do.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Choice-Supportive Bias: Remembering one's choices as better than they were.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Selective Perception: Noticing only things that align with one's expectations.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Blind Spot Bias: Recognizing biases in others but not in oneself.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Negativity Bias: Giving more weight to negative experiences than positive ones.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Outcome Bias: Judging a decision based on the outcome rather than the quality of the decision process.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Clustering Illusion: Seeing patterns in random data.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Pessimism Bias: Overestimating the likelihood of negative outcomes.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Recency Bias: Giving more weight to the most recent information.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Salience Bias: Focusing on the most prominent or emotionally striking information.
nodes.subject: Reading Comprehension

nodes.label: Sentence Equivalence is a test of logical consistency, not just vocabulary.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'blank' is a placeholder for a word that must be supported by the surrounding text.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The two correct answers must create sentences that are synonymous in meaning.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The two correct answers do not need to be synonyms in isolation, only in the context of the sentence.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: Always read the entire sentence before looking at the answer choices.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: Identify the 'clue'—the part of the sentence that dictates the meaning of the blank.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: Identify the 'signpost'—the transition word that dictates the logical flow.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: Predict a word or phrase for the blank before evaluating choices.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: Supportive transitions indicate that the blank should align with the clue.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: Common supportive markers: 'and', 'furthermore', 'moreover', 'additionally', 'likewise'.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: Colon (:) usage: indicates that what follows explains or defines the preceding clause.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: Semicolon (;) usage: often links two independent clauses that reinforce each other.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: Contrast transitions indicate that the blank should be the opposite of the clue.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: Common contrast markers: 'but', 'however', 'yet', 'nevertheless', 'nonetheless'.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: Subordinating contrast markers: 'although', 'even though', 'despite', 'in spite of', 'whereas'.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: Shift markers: 'on the contrary', 'conversely', 'in contrast', 'rather than'.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: Cause-and-effect markers indicate logical progression.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: Markers: 'because', 'since', 'therefore', 'consequently', 'thus', 'as a result'.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: Negative prefixes (un-, in-, non-, dis-) can flip the meaning of a sentence.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: Double negatives (e.g., 'not uncommon') create a positive meaning.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: Definition clues: The sentence provides a direct synonym or explanation for the blank.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: Example clues: The blank is described by a list of specific instances.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: Comparison clues: The blank is linked to another entity via 'like', 'as', or 'similar to'.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: Contrast clues: The blank is defined by what it is not.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: Time-based clues: Words like 'formerly', 'initially', or 'subsequently' set the temporal context.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: Degree clues: Words like 'merely', 'somewhat', or 'entirely' modify the intensity of the blank.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: Subjective clues: Words like 'unfortunately', 'ironically', or 'surprisingly' set the author's tone.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'missing link' clue: The blank is the result of a process described in the sentence.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'parallel structure' clue: The blank must match the grammatical and logical weight of a parallel phrase.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'hidden contrast' clue: Words like 'while' can mean 'although' (contrast) or 'at the same time' (simultaneity).
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'degree of intensity' clue: Distinguishing between 'mildly' and 'severely' based on context.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'author's attitude' clue: Identifying if the author is praising, criticizing, or neutral.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'rhetorical question' clue: The blank answers a question posed by the author.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'quotation' clue: The blank is a word that someone else would use in that situation.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'metaphorical' clue: The blank is part of a figurative comparison.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'causal chain' clue: A leads to B, which leads to the blank.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'omission' clue: The blank represents something that is missing or absent.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'redundancy' clue: The blank is a restatement of a previous idea.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'irony' clue: The blank is the opposite of what is expected in a normal situation.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'nuance' clue: The blank requires a word with a specific connotation (positive/negative).
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'scope' clue: The blank must fit the specific domain (e.g., scientific, historical, personal).
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'part-to-whole' clue: The blank describes a component of a larger system.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'function' clue: The blank describes what an object or person does.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'evolution' clue: The blank describes how something has changed over time.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'limitation' clue: The blank is restricted by a specific condition mentioned in the sentence.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'exaggeration' clue: The blank is part of a hyperbolic statement.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'understatement' clue: The blank is part of a litotes or ironic minimization.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'authority' clue: The blank is supported by an expert opinion.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'evidence' clue: The blank is a conclusion drawn from provided data.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'contradiction' clue: The blank is a paradox that the sentence resolves.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'sequence' clue: The blank is a step in a chronological process.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'comparison' clue: The blank is compared to something else to highlight a quality.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'contrast' clue: The blank is contrasted with something else to highlight a difference.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'definition' clue: The blank is defined by a relative clause.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'appositive' clue: The blank is renamed or described by an appositive phrase.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'prepositional' clue: The blank is modified by a prepositional phrase.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'adverbial' clue: The blank is modified by an adverb of manner or degree.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'adjectival' clue: The blank is modified by an adjective of quality.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'verb' clue: The blank is the action performed by the subject.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'object' clue: The blank is the recipient of the action.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'subject' clue: The blank is the agent of the action.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'conjunction' clue: The blank is linked to another word by a coordinating conjunction.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'correlative' clue: The blank is part of a 'not only... but also' structure.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'modal' clue: The blank is modified by a modal verb (e.g., 'could', 'should').
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'tense' clue: The blank must fit the temporal frame of the sentence.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'voice' clue: The blank must fit the active or passive construction.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'mood' clue: The blank must fit the indicative, subjunctive, or imperative mood.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'number' clue: The blank must agree with the subject in singular or plural.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'gender' clue: The blank must agree with the pronoun reference.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'case' clue: The blank must be in the correct subjective, objective, or possessive case.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'collocation' clue: The blank must form a natural phrase with surrounding words.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'idiom' clue: The blank is part of a fixed idiomatic expression.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'register' clue: The blank must match the formal or informal tone of the passage.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'style' clue: The blank must match the literary or technical style of the passage.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'rhythm' clue: The blank must fit the cadence of the sentence.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'emphasis' clue: The blank is the focal point of the sentence's argument.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'summary' clue: The blank summarizes the preceding argument.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'inference' clue: The blank is a logical deduction from the premises.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'assumption' clue: The blank is a necessary presupposition for the sentence to be true.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'implication' clue: The blank is a consequence of the stated facts.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'generalization' clue: The blank is a broad statement based on specific examples.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'exception' clue: The blank is an anomaly in a general trend.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'comparison' clue: The blank is a point of similarity between two things.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'contrast' clue: The blank is a point of difference between two things.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'cause' clue: The blank is the reason for an event.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'effect' clue: The blank is the result of an event.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'purpose' clue: The blank is the goal of an action.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'method' clue: The blank is the way something is achieved.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'condition' clue: The blank is dependent on a specific requirement.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'concession' clue: The blank acknowledges a counterpoint.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'qualification' clue: The blank limits the scope of a statement.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'clarification' clue: The blank restates an idea for better understanding.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'elaboration' clue: The blank adds detail to a previous point.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'illustration' clue: The blank provides a concrete example.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'classification' clue: The blank categorizes an item.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'definition' clue: The blank provides the meaning of a term.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'etymology' clue: The blank is derived from a specific root word.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'synonym' clue: The blank is a synonym of a word in the sentence.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: The 'antonym' clue: The blank is an antonym of a word in the sentence.
nodes.subject: Sentence Equivalence

nodes.label: Common contrast markers: 'but', 'however', 'yet', 'although', 'despite', 'nevertheless', 'on the contrary'.

nodes.label: Cause-and-effect markers link an action to its logical consequence.

nodes.label: Common markers: 'because', 'since', 'therefore', 'consequently', 'thus', 'as a result', 'so'.

nodes.label: The blank often represents either the cause or the effect described by the sentence.

nodes.label: Direct Definition: The sentence explicitly defines the word needed for the blank.

nodes.label: Synonym Clues: The sentence provides a word with a similar meaning to the blank.

nodes.label: Antonym Clues: The sentence provides a word with an opposite meaning to the blank.

nodes.label: Example Clues: The sentence lists specific instances that define the category of the blank.

nodes.label: Inference Clues: The sentence requires logical deduction based on the scenario provided.

nodes.label: Tone Clues: The author's attitude (positive, negative, neutral) dictates the word choice.

nodes.label: Punctuation Clues: Colons and semicolons often signal that the following clause explains the preceding one.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Punctuation Clues: Dashes often set off parenthetical definitions or clarifications.

nodes.label: The 'Pivot Point': The specific word that changes the direction of the sentence.

nodes.label: Double-Blank Strategy: Solve the easier blank first to narrow down the options for the second.

nodes.label: Triple-Blank Strategy: Look for the 'anchor' blank that is most clearly defined by the context.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: The 'Negative' Trap: Watch for double negatives (e.g., 'not uncommon') which equate to a positive.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: The 'Degree' Trap: Distinguish between words that differ only in intensity (e.g., 'annoyed' vs. 'furious').
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: The 'Part of Speech' Check: Ensure the chosen word grammatically fits the blank.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: The 'Subject-Verb Agreement' Check: Ensure the word choice maintains grammatical consistency.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: The 'Parallelism' Check: Ensure the blank matches the structure of other items in a list.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: The 'Modifier' Check: Ensure the blank is compatible with surrounding adjectives or adverbs.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: The 'Concession' Clause: Phrases like 'While X, Y' imply that X and Y are opposites.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: The 'Comparison' Clause: Phrases like 'Just as X, so Y' imply that X and Y are similar.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: The 'Time' Shift: Words like 'formerly', 'initially', or 'now' indicate a change in state.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: The 'Scope' Check: Ensure the word choice isn't too broad or too narrow for the context.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: The 'Idiomatic' Check: Some words are preferred in specific collocations.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: The 'Nuance' Check: Subtle differences in connotation can eliminate otherwise 'correct' synonyms.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: The 'Red Herring' Trap: Ignore information that is grammatically present but logically irrelevant.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: The 'Extreme' Trap: Avoid words that are too strong unless the context explicitly supports them.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: The 'Neutral' Trap: Avoid words that are too weak if the context demands a strong stance.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: The 'Self-Correction' Check: Re-read the sentence with your chosen word to ensure it flows logically.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: The 'Logic Flow' Check: Does the sentence make sense as a complete thought?
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: The 'Contextual Consistency' Check: Does the word match the author's established persona?
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: The 'Vocabulary Precision' Check: Does the word capture the exact shade of meaning required?
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: The 'Elimination' Strategy: Cross out words that are clearly wrong before selecting the best fit.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: The 'Pairing' Strategy: In two-blank questions, look for pairs that logically relate to each other.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: The 'Contextual Anchor' Strategy: Identify the one word or phrase that dictates the entire sentence's meaning.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: The 'Summary' Strategy: Summarize the sentence in your own words before looking at the choices.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: The 'Clue-to-Blank' Mapping: Draw a mental line between the clue and the blank.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: The 'Signpost-to-Logic' Mapping: Determine if the signpost is a 'plus' or a 'minus' sign.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: The 'Contextual Breadth' Check: Does the sentence imply a broad or narrow scope?

nodes.label: The 'Contextual Depth' Check: Does the sentence require a superficial or profound word?

nodes.label: The 'Contextual Tone' Check: Is the sentence formal, academic, or conversational?

nodes.label: Text Completion is not a vocabulary test, but a logic test using vocabulary as the medium.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: The blank is a placeholder for a word that must be logically supported by the surrounding text.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Avoid 'word association' traps where a word sounds good but lacks textual evidence.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: The 'Clue' is the specific word or phrase that dictates the meaning of the blank.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: The 'Pivot' is the transition word that changes the direction of the sentence logic.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Always predict a word or phrase before looking at the answer choices.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: If you cannot predict a word, look for the relationship (positive/negative) instead.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Eliminate choices that are grammatically incorrect, even if they fit the logic.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Eliminate choices that are logically inconsistent with the sentence's tone.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: The GRE often uses 'distractor' words that are synonyms of each other but incorrect for the context.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Support markers indicate that the blank should align with the rest of the sentence.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Common support markers: 'and', 'furthermore', 'additionally', 'likewise', 'similarly'.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Support markers imply a continuation of the same idea or tone.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Colons (:) act as support markers, indicating that what follows explains or defines what came before.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Semicolons (;) often link two independent clauses that support each other's meaning.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Contrast markers indicate that the blank should be the opposite of the clue.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Common contrast markers: 'although', 'however', 'despite', 'nevertheless', 'on the other hand'.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Contrast markers can signal a shift in time (e.g., 'formerly' vs 'now').
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Contrast markers can signal a shift in perspective (e.g., 'critics argue' vs 'proponents claim').
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: The 'Double Negative' trap: words like 'not' combined with a contrast marker can reverse the logic twice.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Cause and effect markers link an action to its logical consequence.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Common markers: 'because', 'since', 'therefore', 'consequently', 'thus', 'as a result'.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Identify the 'cause' and 'effect' to determine if the blank is the reason or the result.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Watch for 'hidden' cause-effect relationships where the verb implies the result (e.g., 'precipitated', 'engendered').
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Distinguish between a cause and a correlation in the sentence logic.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: In two- or three-blank questions, solve the blank that is most clearly supported first.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Use the solved blank as a new 'clue' to solve the remaining blanks.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Check for internal consistency: do the selected words create a coherent, non-contradictory sentence?
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Do not assume the blanks are independent; they are almost always linked by the sentence's logic.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: If you are stuck, test the combinations of answers to see which pair makes the most sense.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Identify the 'author's attitude' (positive, negative, neutral, or ironic).
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Adjectives often carry strong positive or negative connotations that dictate the blank.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Watch for 'irony' or 'sarcasm' where the author uses words that seem positive but are meant negatively.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Distinguish between 'mild' and 'extreme' versions of words (e.g., 'dislike' vs 'abhor').
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: The GRE favors precise vocabulary; choose the word that fits the specific degree of intensity required.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Avoid 'extreme' answer choices (e.g., 'always', 'never') unless the text explicitly supports them.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Look for 'hedging' language (e.g., 'might', 'could', 'seems') that limits the scope of the sentence.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Recognize 'academic' tone; the GRE rarely uses slang or overly casual language.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Pay attention to 'intensifiers' (e.g., 'truly', 'quite', 'very') that modify the strength of the blank.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Understand the difference between 'denotation' (literal meaning) and 'connotation' (emotional weight).
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Avoid words that sound like the correct answer but have a different meaning.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Example: 'Affect' vs 'Effect' or 'Compliment' vs 'Complement'.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: A word might fit the first half of a sentence but fail the second half.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Always check the entire sentence for consistency before finalizing an answer.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: In multi-blank questions, a word that fits one blank perfectly might make the other blank impossible to fill.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Do not use outside facts to answer the question; rely only on the provided text.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: The GRE tests your ability to process the provided information, not your general knowledge of history or science.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: If a sentence seems factually incorrect based on your knowledge, ignore it and follow the internal logic.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: The 'truth' of the sentence is irrelevant; only the 'logic' of the sentence matters.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: In multi-blank questions, distractors are often synonyms of each other.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: If two options are synonyms, they are likely both incorrect unless the blank requires that specific nuance.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Always look for the word that creates the most precise logical fit, not just a synonym of the clue.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Be wary of 'thesaurus' answers that look sophisticated but don't fit the sentence structure.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Check for 'collocations'—words that naturally go together in English (e.g., 'mitigate damage' vs 'alleviate damage').
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Learn common Latin and Greek roots to decode unfamiliar words.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Example: 'Bene-' (good), 'Mal-' (bad), 'Chron-' (time), 'Dict-' (speak).
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Prefixes change the meaning (e.g., 'un-', 'dis-', 're-', 'pre-').
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Suffixes indicate the part of speech (e.g., '-tion' is a noun, '-ive' is an adjective).
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Use suffixes to determine if the blank requires a noun, verb, or adjective.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Learn words in groups (synonyms/antonyms) rather than in isolation.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Use flashcards with example sentences rather than just definitions.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Read high-level academic journals (e.g., The Economist, Scientific American) to see words in context.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Keep a 'mistake journal' for words you missed in practice tests.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Review your mistake journal weekly to reinforce memory.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Focus on 'high-frequency' GRE words that appear repeatedly in official materials.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Aim for 60-90 seconds per Text Completion question.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: If a question is taking too long, mark it, guess, and move on.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Don't spend extra time on a question just because you 'almost' have the answer.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Use the 'Mark' feature to return to difficult questions at the end of the section.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Ensure you have time to finish the easier questions at the end of the section.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Eliminate choices that are clearly wrong first to increase your odds of guessing.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Use the 'POE' method even if you think you know the answer to confirm your choice.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: If you are down to two choices, re-read the sentence with both to see which flows better.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Look for 'logical deal-breakers' that make an answer choice impossible.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Don't be afraid to eliminate all but one choice and select it, even if you don't know the definition of the remaining word.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Identify 'signpost' words that indicate the author's argument structure.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Recognize 'concession' clauses (e.g., 'while it is true that X, Y is still the case').
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Understand 'logical equivalence'—when two parts of a sentence must mean the same thing.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Identify 'comparative' structures (e.g., 'more X than Y') that set up a relationship between two terms.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Watch for 'negation' words (e.g., 'hardly', 'scarcely', 'rarely') that flip the meaning of a sentence.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Analyze the 'scope' of the sentence—does it apply to a specific group or a general population?
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Look for 'evidence-based' conclusions where the blank is a logical deduction from the provided evidence.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Identify 'rhetorical' questions that imply their own answer.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Understand how 'modifying phrases' (appositives) provide clues for the blank.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Practice 'deconstructing' long, complex sentences into smaller, manageable clauses.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Identify the 'main verb' of the sentence to understand the primary action or state.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Watch for 'parallelism'—where two parts of a sentence follow the same grammatical and logical structure.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Recognize 'conditional' statements (e.g., 'if X, then Y') that dictate the relationship between blanks.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Understand 'temporal' markers (e.g., 'initially', 'subsequently', 'meanwhile') that establish a timeline.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Identify 'subject-verb' agreement to ensure the chosen word fits the grammatical requirements.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Look for 'pronoun references' that link back to earlier nouns, providing clues for the blank.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Practice 'rephrasing' the sentence in your own words to simplify the logic.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Be aware of 'idiomatic' expressions that require specific prepositions or word pairings.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Understand 'degree' markers (e.g., 'somewhat', 'entirely', 'partially') that modify the intensity of the blank.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Recognize 'causal' verbs (e.g., 'fosters', 'hinders', 'prompts') that define the relationship between ideas.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Practice 'active reading'—constantly asking 'why' the author included a specific word or phrase.

nodes.label: Use 'structural analysis' to identify the core argument of the sentence.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Look for 'contrastive' pairs (e.g., 'theory vs practice', 'ideal vs reality') that are common in GRE questions.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Understand 'concessive' markers (e.g., 'granted', 'admittedly') that acknowledge an opposing view before refuting it.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Practice 'predictive modeling'—guessing the blank based on the logical structure before reading the options.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Identify 'qualifiers' (e.g., 'mostly', 'rarely') that restrict the application of a statement.
nodes.subject: Text Completion

nodes.label: Understand 'inversion'—when the subject and verb are swapped for emphasis, potentially obscuring the clue.

nodes.label: Recognize 'elliptical' constructions where words are omitted but implied by the context.

nodes.label: Practice 'logical mapping'—drawing a quick diagram of the relationships between the parts of the sentence.

nodes.label: Understand 'negation of a negation'—when two negative words create a positive meaning.
